text.title: Message Queue Patterns and Broker Selection
text.summary: Point-to-point, pub/sub, request/reply, and dead-letter queue patterns; broker selection guide by guarantee and scale.

section.title: Work Queue (Point-to-Point)
section.p: One producer, one consumer group. Each message processed by exactly one consumer. Best for background jobs: email, image processing, report generation.

section.title: Publish/Subscribe (Fan-out)
section.p: One producer, multiple independent consumer groups. Each group receives every message. Best for event-driven fan-out: order-placed to invoice + shipping + analytics.

section.title: Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)
section.p: Messages that exceed max-receive-count are routed to the DLQ instead of being re-queued infinitely. DLQ must have an alert on depth > 0 and a replay process.

rule.statement: Every queue must have a paired DLQ with max-receive-count 3-5. Add an alert when DLQ depth exceeds 0. Without a DLQ, failed messages retry until they block the queue or are silently discarded.
rule.rationale: Poison messages block FIFO queues indefinitely when there is no DLQ. Without an alert, the DLQ becomes a silent black hole where failed messages accumulate unnoticed.

rule.statement: Pick the broker by the weakest guarantee you can accept: Redis Streams for lightweight at-least-once with consumer groups; RabbitMQ for flexible routing, DLX, and TTL; SQS for fully managed with built-in DLQ; Kafka only when ordered replay across multiple consumer groups is critical.
rule.rationale: Kafka adds significant operational overhead (partition rebalancing, KRaft/ZooKeeper management). For most solo SaaS workloads, Redis Streams or RabbitMQ provide sufficient guarantees at lower operational cost.

list.item: Redis Streams / Valkey: lightweight, consumer groups, ack model. Already running on faion-net.
RabbitMQ: mature, DLX + TTL patterns, flexible routing (direct/topic/fanout/headers).
AWS SQS: fully managed, Standard (at-least-once) or FIFO (exactly-once ordered), built-in DLQ.
Celery (broker: Redis/RabbitMQ): Python task queue with chains, groups, chords, beat scheduler.
Kafka: pick only when ordered replay across N independent consumer groups is the core requirement.
